title: Self-Adapting Network Relaxations for Weakly Coupled Markov Decision Processes
abstract: High-dimensional weakly coupled Markov decision processes (WDPs) arise in dynamic decision making and reinforcement learning, decomposing into smaller Markov decision processes (MDPs) when linking constraints are relaxed. The Lagrangian relaxation of WDPs (LAG) exploits this property to compute policies and (optimistic) bounds efficiently; however, dualizing linking constraints averages away combinatorial information. We introduce feasibility network relaxations (FNRs), a new class of linear programming relaxations that exactly represents the linking constraints. We develop a procedure to obtain the unique minimally sized relaxation, which we refer to as self-adapting FNR, as its size automatically adjusts to the structure of the linking constraints. Our analysis informs model selection: (i) the self-adapting FNR provides (weakly) stronger bounds than LAG, is polynomially sized when linking constraints admit a tractable network representation, and can even be smaller than LAG, and (ii) self-adapting FNR provides bounds and policies that match the approximate linear programming (ALP) approach but is substantially smaller in size than the ALP formulation and a recent alternative Lagrangian that is equivalent to ALP. We perform numerical experiments on constrained dynamic assortment and preemptive maintenance applications. Our results show that self-adapting FNR significantly improves upon LAG in terms of policy performance and/or bounds, while being an order of magnitude faster than an alternative Lagrangian and ALP, which are unsolvable in several instances.

author.Selvaprabu Nadarajah: Information and Decision Sciences, University of Illinois Chicago, Chicago, Illinois 60607
author.Andre A. Cire: Department of Management, University of Toronto Scarborough & Rotman School of Management, Toronto, Ontario M5S 1A1, Canada

article.1. Introduction: Markov decision processes (MDPs) define a fundamental model class in dynamic decision making and reinforcement learning. Weakly coupled MDPs (WDPs) represent an important and structured subclass that arises in a number of applications, including multiarmed bandits (Whittle 1988,Balseiro and Gur 2019), inventory routing and dispatch (Papadaki and Powell 2003,Adelman 2004,Kleywegt et al. 2004), marketing (Bertsimas and Mersereau 2007), and healthcare (D’Aeth et al. 2023), to name a few. Specifically, a WDP is composed of a finite number of “component” MDPs linked by linear constraints that involve actions across multiple such MDPs. We briefly discuss this structure using two examples. Multiarmed bandit problems: A retailer needs to construct an assortment at each period over a finite planning horizon by choosing from a fixed set of products (i.e., arms). Adding a product to the assortment (i.e., an arm pull) gives the retailer access to true demand data and improves existing knowledge about the demand for this product. The goal is to maximize the sum of assortment revenues over the planning horizon by choosing arms that balance (i) the learning of demand and the resulting improvement in future assortment revenues (i.e., exploration) with (ii) the maximization of the current period’s assortment revenue using existing knowledge (i.e., exploitation). Each component MDP captures this trade-off for a specific product. Component MDPs are linked by restrictions on which subsets of arms can be pulled, for example, representing a limited budget. Multiarmed bandits arise in many areas beyond assortment planning, for instance, advertising and healthcare. We refer toBertsekas (2017)andSlivkins (2019)for further details and variants. Preemptive maintenance of assets: In each period, a subset of deteriorating assets can be preemptively maintained by incurring a cost, reducing their probability of failure in the near future. Not maintaining an asset increases its probability of failure. If an asset fails, a repair cost that exceeds the preemptive maintenance cost is incurred. Component MDPs capture the stochastic deterioration of assets and the effect of maintenance. Assets that can be maintained simultaneously are typically restricted because of risk considerations. For instance, if too many assets are down for maintenance in the same region, this may affect service. These restrictions constitute linking constraints. The goal of the decision maker is to balance preemptive maintenance costs with reactive repair costs in the event of a failure to minimize expected total costs. Such problems arise in maintaining transportation and telecommunications systems, the electricity grid, and other energy assets (Merizalde et al. 2019,de Jonge and Scarf 2020). WDPs arising in practical problems are hard to solve because their state and action spaces are large. Nevertheless, the structure of a WDP ensures that relaxing the linking constraints decomposes the problem into easier-to-solve component MDPs. This property has motivated the use of Lagrangian relaxation (LAG) to efficiently approximate WDPs and obtain (optimistic) bounds and value-function approximations (VFAs). The LAG VFA is the dual optimal solution of a linear program that models the individual component MDPs exactly, but only enforces an expectation of the linking constraints (Hawkins 2003). This “averaging” can result in the loss of important combinatorial information that may be needed to obtain better VFAs and bounds. VFAs are important from a decision-making perspective because they are used in a separate so-called greedy optimization to define policies (Adelman and Mersereau 2008,Bertsimas and Mišić 2016). In this paper, we propose a novel class of linear programming relaxations to WDPs, which we dub feasibility network relaxations (FNRs), that strengthen the Lagrangian relaxation. The key idea is to replace the expectation of the linking constraints in the linear program associated with LAG with an exact flow representation of these constraints. Underlying this flow representation is a “feasibility network” with as many layers as component MDPs, and each of its paths represents a feasible state-action pair of the WDP and vice versa. We show that the optimal objective function value of any FNR provides a bound that is no worse than the LAG bound, and we illustrate that the former bound can substantially improve on the latter one. We can also derive a VFA associated with an FNR by considering the dual optimal solution. There are multiple network encodings of the linking constraints, each leading to FNRs with the same VFA but differing in formulation size (i.e., the number of variables and constraints). An FNR’s size depends on the size of the network underlying its formulation. A natural way to build a feasibility network is to use its nodes to store the remaining slacks in the linking constraints as component-MDP actions are taken at each layer. When linking constraints represent resource capacities, this network can be interpreted as tracking resource availability levels, with slacks representing leftover capacities. We show that this slack-based network, although intuitive, may not be of minimal size, and develop a procedure to find the minimally sized network, which we prove is unique. Our procedure starts with the slack-based network and eliminates redundancies by merging nodes. Therefore, the minimal network can be interpreted as representing unions of interchangeable resource availability levels. We refer to FNR that embeds this minimal network as self-adapting FNR because its size automatically adapts to the structure of the linking constraints on a given problem instance. We analyze self-adapting FNR to inform model selection with respect to existing methods that approximate WDPs. LAG:We show that self-adapting FNR and LAG are equivalent in terms of their bounds and VFAs if a unimodularity condition holds for the flow representation of the averaged linking constraints used in the LAG linear program. In this case, there is no quality advantage in using self-adapting FNRs. However, this equivalence is not the norm. We provide classes of multiarmed bandit and maintenance problem instances for which self-adapting FNR can substantially improve upon the LAG bound while being polynomially sized (i.e., the size is a polynomial function of the number of component MDPs, the number of periods of these MDPs, and the state and action space sizes). We also show that FNR might be smaller than LAG for instances with a large number of linking constraints. This potentially surprising result happens because FNR is an adaptive model with a size proportional to the difficulty of an instance, whereas LAG, although a weaker relaxation, is a nonadaptive model with a size that increases with the number of constraints but is not a function of the instance difficulty. Approximate Linear Programming (ALP):We establish that the self-adapting FNR VFA and bound are equivalent to the ones obtained using ALP (Schweitzer and Seidmann 1985,de Farias and Van Roy 2003), which solves a different linear programming model. In the WDP literature, it is known that the ALP VFA is superior to the LAG VFA, but the emerging consensus is that the difficulty in computing the ALP VFA limits its practical viability (Adelman and Mersereau 2008,Bertsimas and Mišić 2016,Brown and Zhang 2022). We show that the self-adapting FNR is smaller than the ALP model for all practical purposes, even polynomially sized for problem classes for which the ALP model is exponential in size. Similarly, we show that FNR is smaller in size than a recent Lagrangian variant proposed byBertsimas and Mišić (2016). Once again, these results show that our adaptive FNR formulation is more efficient than nonadaptive models in the literature of equivalent relaxation strength. Our model selection results lead to the following insights. By exploiting instance-specific information in the formulation phase, self-adapting FNR provides a more accurate assessment of the computational difficulty of improving on the LAG approach, whereas existing ALP models for WDPs exaggerate this computational difficulty. As a result, we suggest revisiting the model choice for approximating WDPs as follows: (i) when self-adapting FNR is known to be equivalent to the LAG approach for a problem class, use LAG unless the WDP has a large number of constraints, in which case self-adapting FNR may be faster to solve; and (ii) when the equivalence does not hold, try to solve self-adapting FNR exactly using a commercial solver, and consider LAG only if the solution time is prohibitive. We compare the self-adapting FNR with LAG, ALP, and the alternate Lagrangian approach (ALT-LAG) fromBertsimas and Mišić (2016), which they show to be equivalent to ALP. We test these methods on a (restless) multiarmed bandit problem arising in a constrained dynamic assortment application motivated byCaro and Gallien (2007)and a preemptive maintenance problem based on information from a major telecommunications provider. In both applications, solving ALP was not possible because of its size in our computational infrastructure. We were able to solve the ALT-LAG model for small instances, but the self-adapting FNR was substantially smaller in size, specifically 100 and 10,000 times less on small dynamic assortment and preemptive maintenance instances, respectively, with proportionate improvements in solution times. The policies and bounds of both methods were near-optimal and comparable in these small instances. In large instances, only self-adapting FNR and LAG were computationally viable. For the dynamic assortment application, the self-adapting FNR bound improvements over LAG were between 0% and 60%, and these improvements increased with the number of linking constraints and decreased as these constraints became tighter. The policy performance of both approaches is comparable. For the maintenance application, the self-adapting FNR bound improvement was between 1% and 70%, with greater differences occurring when there were more linking constraints. Here, the self-adapting FNR policy was also 20%–60% better than that of LAG. Self-adapting FNR was slower to solve than LAG on the assortment instances, but neither method dominated the other in terms of solution time on the maintenance instances. 1.1. Related Literature and Contributions Our results add to the growing, although still limited, literature that compares approximate dynamic programming methods for WDPs.Adelman and Mersereau (2008)is a seminal paper that considers ALP and LAG. They show that the ALP bound is tighter than the one from LAG and provide conditions for when these two bounds are equivalent. In particular, they demonstrate this equivalence for restless bandit problems that allow one arm to be pulled at each stage. Numerically, they find that ALP and LAG exhibit similar optimistic bounds and policies, but ALP is much more difficult to solve.Bertsimas and Mišić (2016)show that ALP and ALT-LAG are equivalent for WDPs where the linking constraints are state independent.Brown and Zhang (2022)show that ALP and LAG are equivalent when a unimodularity property holds, which extends the results ofAdelman and Mersereau (2008). Collectively, this literature has the following key takeaways: (i) ALP is equivalent to LAG in special cases, in which case one would use LAG, and (ii) ALP is stronger than LAG in general, but the difficulty of solving the former linear program results in the use of LAG being more practical. The introduction of self-adapting FNR will likely not change takeaway (i), as we expect LAG to be the smallest model for most WDPs in this case, but it does suggest revisiting takeaway (ii). That is, instead of defaulting to LAG one should consider solving self-adapting FNR, as it is much smaller than ALP and even ALT-LAG. Self-adapting FNR is conceptually new to the WDP literature as a linear programming model that adapts to the nature of the instance and, in particular, has a notion of minimality associated with it in terms of size. An additional contribution is our analysis that compares the sizes of FNR, ALP, ALT-LAG, and LAG, which informs model selection. Furthermore, this work also explores an alternative characterization of the difficulty of computing policies. The VFAs computed by LAG and FNR are separable; that is, they are sums of component-specific VFAs. Once such a VFA is available, the associated policy decision at a state can be obtained by solving a greedy optimization, as mentioned earlier. For a single cardinality constraint,Brown and Smith (2020, section 4.1) show that solving the greedy optimization can be simplified because the resulting policy is equivalent to an index policy. For general linking constraints, we show that this optimization can be cast as a longest-path problem on an exact network representation of the linking constraints; thus, its difficulty depends on the size of the minimal network. To the best of our knowledge, such a characterization of difficulty is novel. Our computation of policies and bounds for preemptive maintenance cast as a WDP adds to the growing literature that exploits weak coupling in applications (Caro and Gallien 2007,Marklund and Rosling 2012,Hu and Frazier 2017,Nambiar et al. 2021,D’Aeth et al. 2023).D’Aeth et al. (2023)are notable here for considering fluid approximations. 1.2. Paper Structure The paper is organized as follows. InSection 2, we provide background information on WDPs and LAG. We introduce feasibility networks and FNRs inSections 3and4, respectively. Model selection is covered inSection 5. The numerical results are discussed inSection 6. We conclude inSection 7. An electronic companion contains all proofs and supporting material.

Examples We describe two examples to illustrate the elements of a WDP. We will leverage them in our discussions and numerical experiments in later sections. (Multiarmed Bandit). We consider a generalization of the multiarmed bandit application investigated byCaro and Gallien (2007). The problem models a fashion retailer who repeatedly chooses which items to include in an assortment during a sales season. The assortment must adhere to budget constraints (e.g., shelf capacity), and the demand of each item follows a distribution with unknown parameters. At the end of each period, the retailer updates the demand parameters based on the sales of items included in the assortment. The retailer aims to maximize total profits by balancing exploration and exploitation. Many applications in marketing and healthcare have similar structures and are classified more broadly as dynamic selection problems (Brown and Smith 2020). Formally, the problem is defined by a set ofJproducts. The productjhas an associated component MDP with a statesjthat represents the parameters of the prior distribution of its demand (e.g., the rate and shape of a Gamma distribution if demand is assumed to be a negative binomial). The starting state𝒔0s0is an initial estimate of these parameters. Action𝑎𝑗ajcaptures whether itemjis included (aj= 1) or excluded (aj= 0) from the assortment. If this item is added, the retailer obtains an expected profit𝑟𝑗⁡(𝑠𝑗,𝑎𝑗)rj⁡(sj,aj)from the sale ofjand uses the observed demand to update the parameters of the demand prior. Furthermore, at the beginning of each period𝑡∈𝒯t∈T, the assortment must observeKstate-independent budget constraints, which define the action set (Preemptive Asset Maintenance). We investigate an abstraction of the maintenance problem faced by a telecommunications company in North America that periodically performs network update campaigns to avoid system failure due to equipment deterioration (for anonymity, we refer toMahimkar et al. 2019for operational details of such a system; the citation does not include the authors’ name, affiliation, or any identifiable information). A network update campaign specifies the equipment to be maintained weekly during a finite horizon (e.g.,𝑇=14T=14weeks). The deterioration of equipment𝑗∈𝒥j∈Jand the decision to perform maintenance are formulated as a component MDP. The linkage between these component MDPs arises because there are restrictions on which equipment pairs can be maintained in the same week. In our telecommunication application, this constraint is due to hardware and software considerations. For example, two servers sharing a network rack cannot be updated in the same week, to ensure that there is redundancy in case the update fails. The statesjof thej-th component MDP in weektis a scalar that captures the number of weeks elapsed since the last maintenance. The action𝒂=(𝑎1,…,𝑎𝐽)a=(a1,…,aJ)represents the maintenance decisions across equipment, with𝑎𝑗ajequal to one if equipmentjis maintained in weektand zero otherwise. The pairwise restrictions on maintenance decisions are formulated via a state-independent action set If equipmentjis maintained during weekt(that is,𝑎𝑗=1aj=1), the next period state𝑠′𝑗sj′is reset to zero. Otherwise (i.e.,𝑎𝑗=0aj=0),𝑠′𝑗sj′equals𝑠𝑗+1sj+1. At the end of each week, there is a positive probability of the equipment failing, which is a function of the state, unless the equipment is maintained during the week, in which case the probability is zero. Following well-known models in the maintenance literature (van Noortwijk 2009), the deterioration follows a Gamma process, and failure occurs when its marginal value in weekt, which has a Gamma distribution, exceeds an equipment-specific threshold. In particular, the shape parameter of this Gamma distribution is an increasing function of𝑠𝑡,𝑗st,j; that is, the probability of failure increases as the equipment is not maintained for a longer duration. 2.3. A network representation admits a linear formulation with variables and constraints proportional to its size (i.e., the sum of the node and edge set cardinalities), which can replace (9) inD-LAGto define a stronger relaxation, as we will see inSection 4. Feasibility Network Relaxations: The central idea to obtain a stronger relaxation is to replace Constraints (9) in the Lagrangian flow formulationD-LAGwith constraints that ensure the component MDP flows adhere to the slack or minimal networks, which we generically refer to as a “feasibility network” going forward. In other words, we strengthen the averaged version of the linking constraints inD-LAGby instead imposing these constraints exactly in every period via a flow representation of the feasibility network. InSection 4.1, we present the linear programming relaxation and bound based on a feasibility network. InSection 4.2, we describe the associated policy and computation. InSection 4.3, we provide an illustration. 4.1. Next, consider the preemptive maintenance application formulated inExample 2. In contrast, FNR(SL) need not be compact.□□ We note that even if compactness cannot generally be guaranteed for a problem class, FNR(SA) may still be tractable to solve using a commercial solver (Gurobi Optimization LLC 2022) for several instances of the problem, as its size adapts to the data and structure of the specific linking constraints. We present the infinite-horizon version ofFNRin EC.2 in the electronic companion. The number of Lagrange multipliers in this case will depend on the size of the minimal network. article.5. Model Selection: In this section, we compareFNRand standard WDP approximations in the literature. Our comparative metrics are the quality of the optimistic bound and policies as well as the size of the linear programming formulations. InSection 5.1, we compareFNRwith the Lagrangian relaxation model fromSection 2.3, or more precisely with its dualD-LAG. InSection 5.2, our benchmark is the ALP approach (de Farias and Van Roy 2003). 5.1. Lagrangian Relaxation We begin by discussing the conditions under which the bounds and policies ofLAGandFNRcoincide. Consider the linear description of the feasible component state-action pairs ofLAGper period, augmented with a redundant inequality that states that the total flow of the system is one, (Equivalence BetweenLAGandFNR).If the constraint matrix𝑀LAG𝑡MtLAGis totally unimodular (TU) for each period𝑡∈𝒯t∈Tand the right-hand-side vectorbis integral, then the systemsD-LAGandFNRare equivalent; that is, there is a bijection between the feasible (optimal) solution sets ofD-LAGandFNR. Moreover, the following hold: (i)𝑧LAG=𝑧FNRzLAG=zFNR. (ii)A LAG greedy policy is also an FNR greedy policy. From a model selection perspective, when𝑀LAG𝑡MtLAGis TU,LAGis the preferable model, as it leads to the same bounds and policies asFNRand is often smaller in size as discussed later in this subsection. Corollary 2shows that the desired TU property outlined inProposition 6holds for the cardinality bandit problem discussed inExample 1, which has a single cardinality constraint limiting the number of arm pulls. For example,Adelman and Mersereau (2008)andBertsimas and Mišić (2016)tackle a version of this problem with one arm pull (i.e., right-hand-side value of one), which we generalize here. For a cardinality bandit, theD-LAGandFNRsystems are equivalent. For many problems, the TU property mentioned above does not hold, and theFNRbound and the policy can improve upon theLAGbound; that is, the inequality inProposition 4is strict. This was illustrated for the knapsack bandit inSection 4.3. It is also true for the preemptive maintenance problem outlined inExample 2because the underlying matrix is an independent set system. For such problems, the practical viability of achieving a bound/policy improvement usingFNRdepends on the computational burden required to solve its specification based on the chosen feasibility network, which was discussed inSection 4.1. Intuitively, we expectD-LAGto be smaller in size thanFNRin most applications because the former model captures the linking constraints in expectation, whereas the latter model does so exactly. Formally, the numbers of variables and constraints ofD-LAGare𝒪⁡(𝑇𝐽𝑆𝐴)O⁡(TJSA)and𝒪⁡(𝑇𝐽𝑆)O⁡(TJS), respectively. Recall fromSection 4.1that the analogous numbers forFNRare𝒪⁡(𝑊𝑇𝐽𝑆𝐴)O⁡(WTJSA)and𝒪⁡(𝑊𝑇𝐽𝑆)O⁡(WTJS)whereWdenotes the number of nodes of the largest layer. Both model sizes increase linearly withT, withD-LAGalso exhibiting linear growth inJandK. The size ofFNRmay increase superlinearly inJbecause of the presence ofW. However, its size is interestingly not explicitly dependent onK. Therefore, it is plausible thatFNRis smaller thanD-LAGfor applications with largeK(i.e., many linking constraints) when the network size needed for the representation of feasible state-action pairs is dominated by the number of linking constraints, as shown inExample 9. We also observe this behavior in our numerical study for the preemptive maintenance application. Consider an instance of the preemptive asset maintenance problem presented inExample 2withJ= 10 pieces of equipment and𝑇>1T>1periods. The set of equipment is made up of two subsets𝒥1={1,2,3}J1={1,2,3}and𝒥2={4,…,10}J2={4,…,10}. For any period𝑡∈𝒯t∈T, the equipment in𝒥1J1cannot be maintained simultaneously with any equipment in𝒥2J2. Similarly, no distinct pairs of equipment in𝒥2J2can be maintained at the same time. Recall that actions are binary and state independent; that is, they can be written as𝒜𝑡,𝑗={0,1}At,j={0,1}. Upon inspection ofD-LAG, its size in periodtis Figure 6depicts the minimal network for this instance, where the arcs drawn in thej-th layer are placeholders for a collection of arcs, one for each state in𝒮𝑡,𝑗St,j. Based onFigure 6, the size of FNR(SA) is (see proof ofProposition 8for details): The intuition behind this difference is thatKgrows quadratically with|𝒥2||J2|as the number of componentsJincreases, whereas the size of the minimal network only grows linearly withJ. Therefore, the size ofD-LAGbecomes larger than the size ofFNRfor a sufficiently largeJand the size difference becomes arbitrarily large asJincreases. A practical strategy to determine the tractability of an instance involves using the minimal network construction outlined inSection 3.2, formulating FNR(SA), and solving it using commercial optimization software. In fact, the preemptive maintenance problem instances we consider in our numerical study have a generic conflict graph for which we cannot establish that FNR(SA) is polynomially sized. Nevertheless, FNR(SA) is solvable using a linear programming solver on those instances in reasonable time, is much smaller in size than FNR(SL), and in some cases is also smaller thanD-LAG. Our model selection insight can be summarized as follows: (i) WhenFNRis known to be equivalent toD-LAGfor a problem class (Proposition 6), solve the smaller of two modelsLAGand FNR(SA), which is likelyLAG; (ii) When these models are distinct, solve FNR(SA) exactly using a commercial solver and considerLAGonly if the FNR(SA) solution time is prohibitive. 5.2. Approximate Linear Programming ALP (Schweitzer and Seidmann 1985,de Farias and Van Roy 2003) projects the variable space of the exact linear programming reformulation (PLP) of WDP on the space of separable value function approximations𝑉𝑡⁡(𝒔)≈∑𝑗∈𝒥^𝑉𝑡,𝑗⁢(𝑠𝑗)Vt⁡(s)≈∑j∈JV^t,j⁢(sj). We focus on the dual of the associated linear program because it is most easily comparable toFNR(please see EC.1 in the electronic companion for the primal formulation), which is The number of constraints inALPfor each period is the sum of the component state space sizes. It is thus compact. However, the variables𝑥𝑡⁡(𝒔,𝒂)xt⁡(s,a)still depend on the full WDP state space and action space cardinalities and hence grow exponentially large in number withJ.ALPfor WDP is known to provide stronger bounds thanLAGbut is difficult to solve (Adelman and Mersereau 2008), which has contributed to its limited use. Let𝑧ALPzALPdenote the optimal objective value ofALP. The ALP VFA is defined by the optimal values of the dual variables associated with Constraints (26).Proposition 7establishes that the VFAs and bounds of ALP andFNRcoincide. It follows that these models are also equivalent in terms of their policies. TheALPVFA coincides with theFNRVFA for any feasibility network. Moreover, we have𝑧ALP=𝑧FNRzALP=zFNR. Despite the quality equivalence,ALPandFNRcan differ drastically in terms of size, and hence, solution effort. To shed light into the differences, note that there is a one-to-one equivalence between a state-action pair(𝒔,𝒂)(s,a)in the definition of𝑥𝑡⁡(𝒔,𝒂)xt⁡(s,a)and a root-terminal path in a feasibility network𝒩𝑡Ntfor any periodt. By exploiting instance structure, self-adapting network approximations lead to a significant reduction in sizes because state-action pairs are not represented distinctly; for example, inExample 7the network is compact butℱ𝑡ℱtis exponentially large. To formalize the differences across the two models,Proposition 8provides a period-wise size comparison for a special case of WDPs referred to as decomposable MDPs (Bertsimas and Mišić 2016), which includes both applications exemplified inSection 4.3. We do not count the nonnegativity constraints as they are handled implicitly in solvers (however, we note that their inclusion does not change the qualitative conclusions). (ii)There exists a sequence of decomposable MDP instancesℳ0=ℳ,ℳ1,ℳ2,…,ℳ𝑛M0=M,M1,M2,…,Mn,where𝑛∈𝒪⁢(|𝒱𝑡|+𝐽⁢max𝑗∈𝒥⁡|𝒜𝑡,𝑗|)n∈O⁢(|Vt|+J⁢maxj∈J⁡|At,j|),such that the size ratio#(𝑡)𝐴𝐿𝑃(ℳ𝑖)/#(𝑡)𝐹𝑁𝑅(ℳ𝑖)#ALP(t)(Mi)/#FNR(t)(Mi)is strictly increasing in𝑖=1,2,…,𝑛i=1,2,…,nand Part (i) suggests that FNR(SA) is smaller in size thanALPunder a very mild condition likely true in most applications. Part (ii) provides an upper bound on the ratio of theALPand FNR(SA) model sizes, which shows that theALPsize depends on the product of the state-action space sizes across components whereas theFNRsize relies on the sum of these quantities. Therefore,ALPcan be substantially larger than FNR. Moreover, there is a sequence of instances where this ratio gets worse and achieves the upper bound. Bertsimas and Mišić (2016)propose a different linear programming formulation for decomposable MDPs that matches theALPbound. They construct an equivalent but extended action space by replicating the full action space𝒜𝑡Atfor each component MDP𝑗∈𝒥j∈Jand adding a simpler linking constraint requiring the action vectors to be the same, that is, TheBertsimas and Mišić (2016)approximation is the Lagrangian relaxation obtained when relaxing the equality constraint in̅𝒜𝑡At¯(see EC.1 in the electronic companion for the formulation). As before, to facilitate a comparison with FNR, we focus on the dual of this model, referred to as theALT-LAG: The benefit of this formulation is that, by exploiting the fact that actions are state independent, the flow variables𝑥𝑡,𝑗⁡(𝑠𝑗,𝒂𝑗)xt,j⁡(sj,aj)are linear in both the component state spaces, as well as in the extended action space𝒜𝑡At. Let#(𝑡)𝐴𝐿𝑇𝐿𝐴𝐺(ℳ)#ALTLAG(t)(M)denote the number of variables plus the number of constraints (except nonnegativity) associated with period𝑡∈𝒯\{0}t∈T\{0}ofALT-LAG.Proposition 9compares the sizes ofALT-LAGand FNR(SA). Given a decomposable MDPℳM,the following hold: (i)#(𝑡)𝐴𝐿𝑇𝐿𝐴𝐺(ℳ)≥#(𝑡)𝐹𝑁𝑅(ℳ)#ALTLAG(t)(M)≥#FNR(t)(M). (ii)There exists a sequence of decomposable MDP instancesℳ0=ℳ,ℳ1,ℳ2,…,ℳ𝑛M0=M,M1,M2,…,Mn,where𝑛∈𝒪⁢(|𝒱𝑡|+𝐽⁢max𝑗∈𝒥⁡|𝒜𝑡,𝑗|)n∈O⁢(|Vt|+J⁢maxj∈J⁡|At,j|),such that the size ratio#(𝑡)𝐴𝐿𝑇𝐿𝐴𝐺(ℳ𝑖)/#(𝑡)𝐹𝑁𝑅(ℳ𝑖)#ALTLAG(t)(Mi)/#FNR(t)(Mi)is strictly increasing in𝑖=1,2,…,𝑛i=1,2,…,nand Part (i) establishes thatALT-LAGis larger in size than FNR(SA). Part (ii) shows that this difference can be substantial by bounding the model size ratio. In particular, there is a sequence of instances where the size ofALT-LAGrelative to FNR(SA) increases and achieves the bound. This conveys the intuition thatALT-LAGis a less efficient model for large action spaces because its size can only be bounded by the product of the action space sizes across components, whereas the bound on the size of FNR(SA) depends on the sum of these quantities. The framework and comparison between FNR(SA),ALP, andALT-LAGhighlight that initial effort to find smaller formulations (e.g., via FNR(SA)) has the potential to significantly reduce computational complexity compared with larger ones (e.g.,ALPorALT-LAG). Conceptually, the reasoning behind this advantage is that bothALPandALT-LAGare nonadaptive formulations. That is, given an instance, their formulations do not depend on the data or structure of the instance, whereasFNRis adaptive and exploits such structure to provide a formulation that is a proportionate response to the difficulty of the particular instance being tackled. This is conceptually new to the WDP literature. From a model selection perspective, this suggests revisiting WDPs for which computingALPorALT-LAGbounds and policies was considered to be too expensive and checking if FNR(SA) is solvable for these instances. Finally, recent work byBrown and Zhang (2022)also tackles model selection betweenALPandD-LAG, to be precise, their respective primal formulations. They provide an equivalence result between these two models that is based on a TU property of linking constraints similar toProposition 6, but they require the linking constraint coefficients to be state independent.Propositions 6and7together show that the equivalence betweenALPandLAGholds under essentially the same TU characterization even with state dependence. This is possible because our proofs rely on the fact that replacing Constraint (9) inD-LAGwith the flow formulation (19)–(20) of the feasibility network results in the exact satisfaction of the linking constraints. In contrast,Brown and Zhang (2022)do not leverage this feasibility network perspective.
article.6. Numerical Study: In this section, we compare the methods for approximating WDPs discussed in previous sections for instances of knapsack bandits and preemptive asset maintenance. We consider the health status of equipmentjto be given by a Gamma distribution with shape parameter 7 and scale parameter 1.0, which yields a high likelihood (≥≥80%) of equipment failure within two periods without maintenance. Recall that the action set determining the pairs of equipment that cannot be maintained simultaneously is defined by a conflict graph. We generate an Erdős-Renyi random conflict graph by sampling an edge representing a maintenance conflict between two pieces of equipment with a probability of𝜌conflictρconflict. We refer to this parameter as the conflict probability. Lower conflict probabilities lead to sparser conflict graphs, fewer linking constraints, and larger action spaces. In particular, unlike the knapsack bandit where the number of linking constraints is fixed a priori, the number of linking constraints in the maintenance problem varies based on the conflict graph. article.7. Conclusions: We introduce a new class of relaxations for WDPs that is based on an exact network representation of the linking constraints. The flow representation of this network can be embedded in a linear program to obtain a so-called FNR that provides VFAs and bounds for WDPs that are superior to those of the well-known Lagrangian approach. There are multiple FNRs for the same WDP that match in terms of VFA quality but differ in size, depending on how the linking constraints are encoded in a network. We develop a procedure to construct the FNR of minimal size, which we dub self-adapting FNR because its size (numbers of variables and constraints) adapts to the data and structure of the linking constraints. We establish that this model can be polynomially sized even when its VFA and bounds are superior to those of LAG. Moreover, its VFA matches the ALP VFA, although we show that its size can be substantially smaller than that of ALP, than that of a recent Lagrangian variant that is known to be equivalent to ALP, and in some special cases even than that of LAG. These theoretical results underscore the value of adaptive formulations. For instances of constrained dynamic assortment and preemptive maintenance applications, we show that the self-adapting FNR formulation can be orders of magnitude smaller in size than ALP and the Lagrangian variant mentioned above. As a result, we can solve the first, second, and third models for all instances, none of the instances, and small instances, respectively. The bounds and/or policies of self-adapting FNR can significantly outperform those from the standard Lagrangian approach. Our findings motivate several directions for future research, of which we state two. The first is to develop specialized solution techniques to solve FNR(SA) more efficiently and on a larger scale than is possible using an off-the-shelf commercial solver. Here it would also be interesting to explore the impact of applications where the linking constraints are state dependent, for example, inventory management with storage constraints. The second is to investigate formulations that are even stronger than FNR, potentially allowing for nonseparable terms in the value function by considering the interaction between pairs of components. Efficient solution techniques would be key here as well.
